text.title: MoSCoW Process, Agent Workflow, and Worked Examples
text.summary: Six-step MoSCoW process, capacity validation, agent workflow with prompt patterns, worked examples, and agent gotchas.

section.title: Process Steps

list.item: Step 1: List all requirements from stakeholders, user research, technical needs, competitive analysis
Step 2: Define the timebox — fixed sprint capacity or release budget in person-days
Step 3: Categorize each item using the must-test (fails without? workaround exists? legal/contractual?)
Step 4: Validate effort proportions — Must + Should must not exceed 80% of capacity
Step 5: Stakeholder review — share, discuss disagreements, document decisions and rationale
Step 6: Lock Must and Won't; Should/Could can flex during sprint if capacity changes

section.reference: Release matrix with effort totals per category and capacity summary.
Single-requirement debate template: proposed category, arguments for each, questions, final decision.
Validates 60/20/20 budget split; reports violations when Must is over/under-allocated.

list.item: categorizer agent: maps each requirement to M/S/C/W with rationale and workaround field (required for S/C)
capacity-checker agent: sums effort by bucket, flags violations of 60/20/20 split
reviewer agent: challenges every Must with three test questions; downgrades any that fail all three

rule.statement: Do not let the same agent both propose and categorize requirements. Use proposer → categorizer → reviewer triad.
rule.rationale: An agent that proposes features and then categorizes them has obvious bias toward marking its own proposals as Must. Separation enforces the must-test objectivity.

example.note: Invoice app MVP — 40 person-days capacity, clean 60/20/20 split
example.code:
Must Have (24 days, 60%): User authentication, Create invoice, Send invoice, Basic dashboard
Should Have (8 days, 20%): PDF export (workaround: screenshot), Client management (workaround: manual notes), Payment reminders (workaround: manual follow-up)
Could Have (8 days, 20%): Invoice templates, Recurring invoices, Basic analytics
Won't Have: Mobile app, Team features, Third-party integrations
Outcome: launched on time with Must + most Should complete.

antipattern.description: Agent defaulting to "Should" for ambiguous items without applying the must-test.
antipattern.reason: LLMs hedge on ambiguity. Force must_test booleans (fails_without, no_workaround, legal_or_contractual) as required fields in the output schema; category=="M" only if any is true.

antipattern.description: Agent silently dropping Won't Have from output when the array would be empty.
antipattern.reason: Empty Won't Have is a failure signal, not a valid result. Make it a required non-empty array in the output schema with a minimum of 3 items.

antipattern.description: Agent using raw day counts for effort when given ambiguous requirements.
antipattern.reason: Raw day counts from LLMs are unreliable. Require T-shirt sizes (XS/S/M/L/XL) mapped to day ranges, validated against team past velocity.

rule.statement: Three mandatory human checkpoints: Must lock-in, Won't Have lock-in, any post-lock Must promotion.
rule.rationale: Each of these has downstream scope and expectation consequences. Agents must not apply these changes without explicit human confirmation.
